California
Fair POlitical
Practices Commission
May 3■, ■990
Richard Woo■strum
■8 South Main Street
Lodi′  CA  95240
RE:  Your Request for Adv■
ce
Our File No. A-90-■
88
Dear Mr. woolstrum:
You have requested advice concerning the carnpaign provisions
of the PoliticaL Reform Act of tgla.L You ptan to be a tandidate
for county supervisor in San Joaquin county. AIl of the
conclusions in this letter are based on the assumption that you
are a candidate for elective office.
OUESTION
1. Are ttpublic commentary adsrr placed in a Local newspaper,
which list your name or display your picture, and are paid for by
Glory Properties, Inc., a corporation owned exclusively by you,
contributions to your campaign that are subject to the
contribution limitations?
2. Will the advertiseroents need to be identified by the nane
of your conmittee and its campaign identification 
number?
3, If paying for these advertisernents is considered a
contribution to your campaign, how much may Glory Properties, Inc.
spend after you declare your candidacy?
4. Since Glory Properties, Inc. owes you more than $100,000,
could clory Properties, Inc. continue to pay for the
advertisements over any applicable contribution lirnits and deduct
the excess from the amount o$/ed to you?
5. If the scenario in question #4 above is not allowed, may
you or your campaign pay for the advertisernents as tong as rrpaid
for by...rr and your conmitteers identification 
number appear on
the advertisements?
1 
co..".rrment Code Sections 81OOO-91015. ALI statutory
references are to the covernment Code unless otherwise indicated.
Cornmission regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations
Section 18000, et ses. AII references to regulations ar6 to titl"
2, oivision 6 of the california code of Reguiations.
428 J Strcct,Suitc 800 ● P.0.Box 807 0 Sacran■cnto CA 95804´0807 ● (916)322‐ 5660


Richard Wootstrum
Page fwo
6. If you or your campaign pay for the advertisements, may
their format change to include political 
commentary in support of
or opposition to candidates?
7 . l,lust your carnpa ign committee report , as in-kind
contributions, payments made by Glory Properties, Inc. for
advertisements which appeared in the newspapers before you
declared your candidacy?
8. If your name and photograph t ere removed from the
advertisements, could GLory Properties, Inc. continue to pay for
the advertisements without the payments being considered in-kind
contributions, as long as no opposition or support of candidates
or baLlot measures are included in the commentary?
9. May Glory Properties, Inc. continue to pay for the ads
without the payments being considered in-kind contributions if
their format does not change and your wife, Joani woolstrum, is
Iisted as author?
LO. Are there lirnits on the money or services an individual
or corporation may loan a candidate's carnpaign?
11. May a candidate's cornroittee transfer its debt obligation
to another committee controlled by the sarne candidate?
]-2. May candidates continue to raise money after their
election to pay outstanding debts?
13. Wtrere must the name and campaign identification 
number
of the conmittee appear on brochures, envelopes, postcards, or
business cards?
14. llust the candidate,s voice be a part of any radio or
television advertisement?
15. Uay a non-profit organization you and your wife direct
place advertisements seeking donations for the non-profit
organization, Iisting your naDe as a contact person r.rithout the
cost of the advertisements being considered in-kind contributions
to your campaign?
CONCLUSIONS
l-.. Newspaper advertisenents paid for by your corporation
which.Iist your nane or display yoirr picture- aie in-ki-na
contributions to your canpaign ana arL subject to the rirnitations.
corporate funds are not the personar funds of a candidate, even if
the corporation is who1ly-owned by the candidate. Since a
corporation's Limitation i.s g1,ooo per fiscar v""r ti"iv :. tnrougtr


Richard Woolstrum
Page Three
June 30) per candidate and includes in-kind contributions, GLory
Properties, Inc. may not pay for more than $1,OO0 lrorth of
advertising in a fiscal year. rn addition, Glory properties, rnc.
must cumulate its pa)rments for the advertisements with any other
contributions it makes to your campaign to determine if the 91,ooo
lirnit has been reached.
However, since contributions you make to your own canpaign
are not subject to the contribution ]irnitations, you nay pay for
these advertisements out of your o\rn personal funds without
consideration of ttre Iimits.
2- The Political 
ReforD Act does not require that you or
your comrnittee be identified either by name or identification
number in newspaper advertisenents.
3. After you become a candidate for elective office, Glory
Properties, Inc. may contribute up to 91,000 each fiscaL year to
your candidacy. If cl,ory Properties, Inc. makes no other
contributions to your campaign within a fiscal year, it nay pay
for up to 91,000 in nelrspaper advertisernents to support your
candidacy.
4. clory Properties, Inc. may not pay directly for the
newspaper advertisernents you mention as repa)rment of rnonies owed
to you if the amount Glory Properties, Inc. pays, cumulated with
aII other contributions it rnakes to your campaign, exceeds $1,OOO
in a fiscal year.
5. Your campaign may continue to pay for the advertisements
you mention. ff you choose to pay for the advertisements out of
personal funds, you must first place your funds into the campaign
bank 
_ account prior to expenditure. No identification of your
connittee or its identification 
number is required on any
nerrspaper advertisements.
- 
6.. You may use personal funds to pay for newspaper
advertisements which support or oppose ofttnn candialtls tor
elective office. 
If you place th-se advertisenents for other
candidates at the behest of the other candidates, you nay not
spend more than 91,OOO in the aggregate in a tiscai year per
candidate. If, however, you use personal funds to pia"" --
advertisernents supporting or oppoiing other candidates and do not
rnake the paynents at the behest of the candidates, there are no
limits on how much you may spend. you must ."pori tfr"=" piy^".rt"
on the ALlocation eige of-the Forn 490. rn ad^dition, pi.'ur. ,""
page 39 of the enc]-osed rnfornation ltanual A "orr""r.,i.,g tt"
requirernent to f i1e supprernEnEar rnaEpenaen-E nrp"nai["i" -neports
( Forn 465).


Richard woolstrum
Page Four
If you want to use personal funds to pay for advertisements
r,rhich support your orrn candidacy, you must deposit the funds into
your campaign bank account prior to expenditure. There are no
Iirnits on the amount of your personal funds you may use to support
your orrn election-
7. If Glory Properties, Inc. pays for a nelrspaper
advertisernent rrhich includes your name or photograph with a view
to bringing about your nomination or election to any elective
office, it has made a contribution to you. In such a case, you
are required to report and count tolrard the contribution limit of
$1,ooo any payments made by Glory Properties, Inc. for the
nehrspaper advertisements.
8. As long as you, your campaign, or your opponent(s) are
not identified in the advertisenents, any payments made by clory
Properties, Inc. for the advertisements lrould not be cons j.dered
contributions and, thus, would not be subject to the contribution
l initations .
9. Your wife may be listed as author of the advertisements
without the advertisenents being considered contributions to your
carnpaign.
10. Under the Political Reform Act, Ioans for poJ-itical
purposes are contributions. 
Thus, an individual (other than the
candidate) or corporation must cumulate loans made to a candidate
with any other contribution the individual or corporation has made
to the sane candidate during a fiscal year to deternine if the
contribution limit has been reached for that particuJ.ar candidate.
Personal services are not contributions, unless the person
perforrning the services is paid by a third party. 
In this case,
if an individual pays another person for services rendered to the
canpaign in anticipation of being repaid by the campaign, the
individual has made an in-kind loan to the campaign and must
cumulate the anount paid with any other contributions the
individual has made to the candidate within a fiscal year to
determine if the lirnits have been reached.
11. Debt obligation is not transferable between a
candidaters olrn conmittees. However, contributions received are
transferable between a candidate,s controlled committees. Thus,
funds may be transferred between one controlled committee anal
another committee controrled bv the same candi.date to retire the
debt of the first 
cornrnittee.
t2. 
Candidate-control led committees may continue to raise
money after an election to pay.any outstandiig debts, subject to
the fiscal year contribution lirnitations.


Richard Woolstrum
Page Five
13. The name and address of the committee as listed with the
secretary of State are required to be l-isted in no less than G-
point type on the outside of any mass naiLing (more than 2OO
pieces of sinilar or identical pieces of mail sent in a calendar
ruonth).
There are no identification 
requirernents for business cards,
postcards, brochures, or envelopes, unless they meet the above
definition of a mass nailing.
14. There is no requirenent under the Act that a candidate,s
voice be part of any radio or television advertisement supporting
his or her candidacy. Please contact the Federal Comnunications
Conmission for advice concerning the regulation of radio and
television advertisements under federal Iaw.
15. Under the facts presented, payrnents made by a non-profit
organization for newspaper advertisements containing your name or
photograph are considered in-kind contributions from the non-
profit orgranization to your campaign and must be curnulated with
any other contributions the non-profit organization makes to your
campaign within the same fiscal year to determine if the
contribution limits have been reached. Under the Political 
Reform
Act, non-profit organizations naking politicaJ. contributions for
the first time are linited to $1,000 per candidate per fiscal
year. Please contact the Internal Revenue Service at (8O0) 424-
1040 for any restrictions that nay apply to non-profit
organizations making political contributions.
FA TS
You intend to be a candidate for county supervisor in San
Joaquin County.
You own 10Ot of the stock in clory properties, Inc. This
corporation otlns ttre colden Era Hote1 . clory properties places
I'public comnentariesrr in a local newspaper for business purposes
under your name and that of Golden Era Hotel.
ANALYSIS
rrContributionrr is defined by Section 92015, in part, as Ia
paynglt. . . except to the extent that full and adequate
consideration is received unless it is clear froi the surrounding
circumstances that it is not [ade for poJ.itical prr.po="=. An
expenditure nade at the behest of a candidate... is a contribution
to the candidate... unress fulr and adequate consideration is


Richard woolstrum
Page six
received for making the expenditure. 12 Regulation 18215 defines
political 
purpose as rrinfluencing or attempting to influence the
voters for or against the... 
election of a candidate....tl
Pa)rments rnade at your behest are contributions.
We have previousLy advised that advertisements produced by a
non-profit university to pronote its professional studies program
in which the name, title, 
and photograph of a candidate for
elective office appear, even though no advocacy j.s made, are
contributions to the candidate. (ltccarthv Advice Letter,
No. A-90-163, copy enclosed. )
Since the payments rnade by Glory Properties, Inc. are
controlled by you, its palarents for newspaPer advertisements in
which you are identified are made at.your behest. (Regulation
18215.) Although one of the factors considered in the Mccarthv
advice letter was the advertisernent' s proximity to the candidate's
eJ.ection, the fact that the newspaper advertisements placed by
Glory Properties, Inc. express your political 
views supports the
conclusion that payments made at your behest for the
advertisements are contributions to your campaign.
Furthermore, Section 82007 defines candidate, in pertinent
part, as:
...an individual who...gives his or her consent for
any other person to...make an expenditure lrith a view
to bringing about his or her nomination or election to
elective office, whether or not the specific el-ective
office vrhich he or she niII seek...is known at the tine
the expenditure is made and rrhether or not he or she
has announced his or her candidacy or filed 
a
declaration of candidacy at such tine.
Therefore, payments by Glory Properties, Inc. for
advertisenents of the type described above will be contributions
to you if they rrere made rrith a view to bringing about your
nomination or election to office regardless of whether you have
announced your candidacy or filed a declaration of candidacy.
2 
"l.I.d" at the behestrt neans, in part, ,,a paynent nade under
the controL or at the direction of, in cooperation, consultation,
coordination, or concert with, or at the request or suggestion of
a candidate, controlled conmittee, official 
cornrnittee oi a
political party, or organization forrned or existing prirnarily for
political purposes.'r (Regulation 18215 (b) . )


Richard woolstrum
Page Seven
The Act provides for an exception to the contribution
Iirnitations for the personal funds of a candidate making
contributions to his or her own canpaign. (Section 8530L(b).)
However, funds of a wholly-ormed corporation are not considered to
be the personal funds of the candidate.
A corporation may not contribute more than $1,ooo in a fiscal
year to any one candidate - (section 85301.) section 85L02
includes a corporation in the definition of tiperson.rt
section 85201 requires any personal funds of a candidate to
be deposited into the campaign bank account prior to being
expended for carnpaign purposes.
There is no prohibition against'a candidate using personal
funds to support or oppose other candidates. of course, the
candidate is subject to the contribution lirnits as any other
person under the law. Horrever, if paynents are made out of
personal funds to expressly advocate the nomination, election, or
defeat of any other candidate, and the payments are not made at
the behest of the affected candidate, these pa)rments are
I independent expenditures. and are not subject to the contribution
limits. 
(Section 82031. )
Section 85304 prohibits transfers of any contribution frorn
one candidate to another. Thus, you nay not use your campaign
funds to support another candidate. The trust provisions of
section 85202 states:
A1I contributions deposited into the campaign
account shall be deemed to be held in trust
for expenses associated with the election of
the candidate. . .
This provision prohibits you from using campaign funds to make
contributions or independent expenditures to support or oppose
other candidates for state or local elective office.
Section 84211(j) (5) requires that contributions of glOO or
more nade by the candidate from personal funds in a calendar year
in support or opposition to other candidates or baltot neasures be
itemized. On the Forn 490, these contributions must be reported
on the Allocation Page.
Section 82015 specifies that rvolunteer personal servicesI
are not contributions.


Richard Woolstrum
Page Eight
Even though section 85202 prohibits a candidate from
transferring campaign contributions received for one office to the
same candidate,s campaign connittee for another offj.ce, an order
of the United States District Court, Eastern District of
California enjoins the commission frorn enforcing this provision.
Therefore, contributions received by one committee controlled by a
candidate may be transferred to another comnittee controlled by
the same candidate. (SEIU, AFL-CIO, et aI. v. FPPC, Case No. CM
89-O433 LKK-JFI,!. )
section 84305 provides that
...no candidate or corumittee shall send a mass nailing unless
the name, street address, and city of the candidate or
committee are shown on the outside of each piece of rrail in
the mass mailing. . . .
If you have additional questions, please contact ne at (916)
322-5662.
Sincerely,
Scott Ha1l
Acting
l Counsel-
劣
S. Braaten-Moen
litical 
Reform Consultant
Enclosures
cc: Dennis llorita, CPA

personal funds to expressly advocate the nomination, election, or
defeat of any other candidate, and the payments are not made at
the behest of the affected candidate, these pa)rments are
I independent expenditures. and are not subject to the contribution
limits. 
(Section 82031. )
Section 85304 prohibits transfers of any contribution frorn
one candidate to another. Thus, you nay not use your campaign
funds to support another candidate. The trust provisions of
section 85202 states:
A1I contributions deposited into the campaign
account shall be deemed to be held in trust
for expenses associated with the election of
the candidate. . .
This provision prohibits you from using campaign funds to make
contributions or independent expenditures to support or oppose
other candidates for state or local elective office.
Section 84211(j) (5) requires that contributions of glOO or
more nade by the candidate from personal funds in a calendar year
in support or opposition to other candidates or baltot neasures be
itemized. On the Forn 490, these contributions must be reported
on the Allocation Page.
Section 82015 specifies that rvolunteer personal servicesI
are not contributions.


Richard Woolstrum
Page Eight
Even though section 85202 prohibits a candidate from
transferring campaign contributions received for one office to the
same candidate,s campaign connittee for another offj.ce, an order
of the United States District Court, Eastern District of
California enjoins the commission frorn enforcing this provision.
Therefore, contributions received by one committee controlled by a
candidate may be transferred to another comnittee controlled by
the same candidate. (SEIU, AFL-CIO, et aI. v. FPPC, Case No. CM
89-O433 LKK-JFI,!. )
section 84305 provides that
...no candidate or corumittee shall send a mass nailing unless
the name, street address, and city of the candidate or
committee are shown on the outside of each piece of rrail in
the mass mailing. . . .

If you want to use personal funds to pay for advertisements

7. If Glory Properties, Inc. pays for a nelrspaper
advertisernent rrhich includes your name or photograph with a view
to bringing about your nomination or election to any elective
office, it has made a contribution to you. In such a case, you
are required to report and count tolrard the contribution limit of
$1,ooo any payments made by Glory Properties, Inc. for the
nehrspaper advertisements.
8. As long as you, your campaign, or your opponent(s) are
not identified in the advertisenents, any payments made by clory
Properties, Inc. for the advertisements lrould not be cons j.dered
contributions and, thus, would not be subject to the contribution
l initations .
9. Your wife may be listed as author of the advertisements
without the advertisenents being considered contributions to your
carnpaign.
10. Under the Political Reform Act, Ioans for poJ-itical
purposes are contributions. 
Thus, an individual (other than the
candidate) or corporation must cumulate loans made to a candidate
with any other contribution the individual or corporation has made
to the sane candidate during a fiscal year to deternine if the
contribution limit has been reached for that particuJ.ar candidate.
Personal services are not contributions, unless the person
perforrning the services is paid by a third party. 
In this case,
if an individual pays another person for services rendered to the
canpaign in anticipation of being repaid by the campaign, the
individual has made an in-kind loan to the campaign and must
cumulate the anount paid with any other contributions the
individual has made to the candidate within a fiscal year to
determine if the lirnits have been reached.
11. Debt obligation is not transferable between a
candidaters olrn conmittees. However, contributions received are
transferable between a candidate,s controlled committees. Thus,
funds may be transferred between one controlled committee anal
another committee controrled bv the same candi.date to retire the
debt of the first 
cornrnittee.
t2.

money after an election to pay.any outstandiig debts, subject to
the fiscal year contribution lirnitations.

Page Five

secretary of State are required to be l-isted in no less than G-

pieces of sinilar or identical pieces of mail sent in a calendar
ruonth).
There are no identification 
requirernents for business cards,
postcards, brochures, or envelopes, unless they meet the above
definition of a mass nailing.
14. There is no requirenent under the Act that a candidate,s
voice be part of any radio or television advertisement supporting
his or her candidacy. Please contact the Federal Comnunications
Conmission for advice concerning the regulation of radio and
television advertisements under federal Iaw.
15. Under the facts presented, payrnents made by a non-profit
organization for newspaper advertisements containing your name or
photograph are considered in-kind contributions from the non-
profit orgranization to your campaign and must be curnulated with
any other contributions the non-profit organization makes to your
campaign within the same fiscal year to determine if the
contribution limits have been reached. Under the Political 
Reform
Act, non-profit organizations naking politicaJ. contributions for
the first time are linited to $1,000 per candidate per fiscal
year. Please contact the Internal Revenue Service at (8O0) 424-
1040 for any restrictions that nay apply to non-profit
organizations making political contributions.
